text: In recent years, imaging and three-D virtual models have paved the way towards the knowledge of surgical anatomy. In the urological field, the greatest advantage has been found in the field of nephron sparing surgery. In particular, the newest generation algorithms implemented three-D models are able to identify arterial vascular perfusion volumes within the renal parenchyma. This allowed highly precision selective clamping and minimization of procedure-related ischemic damage. Given the usefulness of this new generation of models, other surgical settings have been explored, such as renal artery aneurysm repair, where knowledge of vascular anatomy is equally crucial. Renal artery aneurysm is a rare entity, which in general consensus must be surgically treated when its diameter crosses two centimeters. The surgical approach may range from endovascular to ex vivo surgery, depending on the aneurysm location, even if in many cases its anatomical complexity requires a tailored approach. In this setting, an extended knowledge of the patient's vascular anatomy can make the difference for a successful procedure.

This video shows the planning and intraoperative management of a right renal prepyellic artery aneurysm treated with robotic assisted surgery with the aid of the technology. The case presented is a thirty-five years old female without comorbidities. She underwent ultrasound and CT scan identifying a multifocal aneurysm of the prepyellic right renal artery located at the level of the renal sinus. a three-D reconstruction of the organ was performed. The renal pedicle appeared to be made of a main artery dividing into three second-order arteries and four third-order branches entering the renal sinus. Among these, the prepialic branch was dilated in its sinusal portion, creating a double aneurysm formation. This latter also showed an arteriovenous shunt with an inferior polar venous branch. On the basis of the information provided by the CT scan and implemented with mathematical algorithms, a new generation model was produced, identifying five different perfusion volumes, of which only one was vascularized by the aneurysmatic artery. Several vascular management strategies were therefore planned.

The surgeon incises the parietal peritoneum to expose the vena cava, main renal vein, and artery, which are suspended on vessel loops. The branches of the vascular pedicle and the parenchymal surface of the kidney are exposed in a centrifugal direction. The inferior segmental polar branch is identified first, followed by the prepyellic branch and its aneurysms. The vascular shunt between the aneurysms and the inferior polar venous branch is isolated. The prepyellic branch is clamped upstream of the aneurysm using a bulldog clamp, leading to rapid emptying of the vessel.

The surgeon uses superficial cautery to demarcate the ischemic mesorenal portion of the kidney following selective clamping. After injecting ICG, vascular flow persists at the lower and upper poles, with visible turbulent venous backflow at the inferior renal vein. The arteriovenous shunt is clipped with hemlock and dissected, excluding the vein from the aneurysmatic cavity, which is opened and progressively dissected from the renal sinus.

The surgeon places an additional hemo-lock on the prepyellic artery below the origin of the fourth-order branch, targeting the upper portion of the mesorenal region. After removing the aneurysmatic sac, a single calyx violation is repaired using a 3-0 PDS suture, and the free margin of the renal sinus is sutured with PDS and biocin, secured with hemolocks.

The surgeon removes the bulldog clamp from the prepyellic branch, reassessing the renal parenchyma and confirming a reduction in the ischemic region on the renal surface. A mild kinking of the prepyellic branch is observed at the clamping site, indicating possible vascular spasm. The artery is gently dabbed with gauze soaked in two percent lidocaine, leading to its physiological distension.

No intraoperative nor postoperative complications were recorded. The patient was discharged on the fourth postoperative day. A three-month follow-up renal function was conserved. Last generation of virtual models represent a new technology for the management of complex vascular anatomies with high risk of surgical complications. With their help, a much more detailed planning of the vessel management strategy and interpretive assistance during all the phases of the procedure is possible, with the goal to minimize interpretive risks while maximizing the functional results.
